narrative:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune is the most powerful man in Congress who used to literally lobby for railroads. Before ascending to the chamber's top seat, Thune served as South Dakota's railroad director, then became a registered lobbyist for the Dakota, Minnesota & Eastern Railroad, pocketing $220,000 to help the company secure a $230 million federal loan. He then ran for Senate — while still lobbying — and won. The revolving door didn't just revolve for Thune; it installed a VIP express lane.

Now presiding over the Senate with the gavel in one hand and decades of corporate relationships in the other, Thune's donor list reads like a Fortune 500 directory. NextEra Energy has pumped nearly $300,000 into his campaigns since 2016. New York Life Insurance has contributed over $150,000. Google, Comcast, AT&T, Verizon, and Sinclair Broadcast Group all write checks. Pharmaceutical CEOs personally donate — Gilead's Daniel O'Day and Pfizer's Albert Bourla each gave $5,800 in a single month in 2022, with pharma companies showering him with over $35,000 in February alone. When Trump's executive order on drug pricing came along, Thune called codifying it "fairly controversial." One wonders for whom.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Thune's record is a masterclass in opposition disguised as thoughtfulness. He voted against the Social Security Fairness Act in December 2024, has floated raising the retirement age, and has used debt ceiling threats to push for cuts to mandatory programs. He opposed net neutrality regulations while cashing telecom checks, calling consumer protections an "internet takeover." On dark money, Thune once admitted he's "never been a big fan of unlimited, undisclosed campaign money" and acknowledged he's "kind of an outlier in my conference" — then did absolutely nothing about it. The DISCLOSE Act? Dead on arrival under his leadership. Congressional stock trading ban? Crickets from the Majority Leader's office.

As a former lobbyist who became the Senate's top Republican, Thune embodies everything the 80% Bill exists to fix: the seamless merger of corporate money, political power, and legislative inaction on wildly popular policies. He's not blocking these reforms because they're bad ideas — he's blocking them because they'd dismantle the system that built his career. South Dakotans sent him to Washington to represent them, but Thune's real constituents have always had corporate letterhead.

Tommy Tuberville arrived in the United States Senate in January 2021 with no prior political experience, having spent his career prowling college football sidelines at Ole Miss, Auburn, Texas Tech, and Cincinnati. He beat incumbent Democrat Doug Jones by double digits on the strength of a Trump endorsement and a folksy "outsider" pitch[1][2]. Since taking office, the Auburn icon has become one of the most financially active members of the Senate, a one-man argument for every reform on the 80% Bill, and an object lesson in what happens when a man who calls himself a public servant primarily serves himself.

On the question of congressional stock trading — supported by more than 90 percent of Americans — Tuberville has been refreshingly honest about where he stands. After violating the STOCK Act 132 times in 2021, disclosing stock and options trades worth between $894,000 and $3.56 million months or years late, he called proposals to ban lawmakers from trading "ridiculous," warning that restrictions would "really cut back on the amount of people that would want to come up here and serve"[3][4]. He has since reported more than 1,300 stock trades — more than any other sitting senator — including defense stocks like Lockheed Martin, IBM, and Honeywell while serving on the Armed Services Committee, and $250,000 in agricultural futures while sitting on the Agriculture Committee[5][6][7].

Tuberville's most famous legislative act was not a bill but a blockade. For nearly ten months in 2023, he placed a blanket hold on over 450 military nominations — including senior commanders and the Joint Chiefs of Staff — to protest a Pentagon policy reimbursing service members for abortion travel costs. The stunt left the Marine Corps without a confirmed commandant during active global instability, drew condemnation from Republican colleagues and military veterans, and was surrendered in December 2023 without a single policy concession[9][10]. The senator who positioned himself as the strongest defender of the military had hollowed out its senior leadership for nearly a year. On economic policy, Tuberville opposes Medicare drug price negotiation, has called Social Security "a scam" warning it will "blow up" while floating private investment accounts as a replacement, and has actively worked to repeal the Corporate Transparency Act, branding it "Big Brother overreach"[13][14][15].

Follow the money and the picture completes itself. Tuberville's top industry donors include finance, insurance, real estate, and defense PACs[12]. There is also the small matter of his residence: property records, campaign finance filings, and travel expense reports all point to a $6 million beachfront estate in Santa Rosa Beach, Florida, not Alabama — a fact that became the subject of a formal residency challenge[16]. Tommy Tuberville ran on draining the swamp and has since done a remarkable impersonation of the swamp. He actively trades in the industries he regulates, opposes the disclosure laws that would make that visible, paralyzed the military in the name of supporting the military, and represents Alabama from a Florida beach house. Where the 80% Bill asks Congress to be accountable to the people who sent them there, Tuberville has been perfectly, transparently accountable to the people who paid for the campaign.

Mike Thompson has represented California's Wine Country in Congress since 1999 -- first the 1st District, now the 4th -- making him one of the longest-serving House Democrats west of the Mississippi. A Vietnam veteran with a Purple Heart and a member of the Blue Dog Coalition, Thompson sits on the powerful Ways and Means Committee, where he serves as Ranking Member of the Tax Subcommittee and a senior member of the Health Subcommittee [1][2]. He is also, not incidentally, the founder of the Congressional Wine Caucus, an organic grape grower who farms twenty acres of sauvignon blanc in Lake County and sells roughly 100 tons a year to Bonterra Vineyards [3][4]. In Washington, they call this "constituent service." In Napa, they call it vertical integration.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Thompson checks several important boxes. He voted against the 2002 Iraq AUMF and has consistently supported repealing both the 2001 and 2002 authorizations, arguing that "Congress has abdicated its role in war activities" [5]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which finally allowed Medicare to negotiate prescription drug prices -- a provision he championed as a Ways and Means member, estimating it saved his district's Medicare beneficiaries $24 million in a single year [6][7]. From his perch on the Tax Subcommittee, he has called out wealthy venture capital, private equity, and real estate firms exploiting loopholes that cost taxpayers over $100 billion, and he supported strengthening IRS enforcement to audit them [8]. He voted for the Elijah E. Cummings Lower Drug Costs Now Act and backed bipartisan drug pricing transparency legislation [9].

Where Thompson gets conspicuously quiet is on the reforms that would inconvenience Thompson. After twenty-seven years in Congress, he has never co-sponsored a term limits bill -- an omission that practically qualifies as a personality trait at this point. He has not been a visible champion of banning congressional stock trading, the DISCLOSE Act, or overturning Citizens United, despite representing a district that would enthusiastically support all three [10]. His challenger in the 2026 Democratic primary, Bay Area financier Eric Jones, has made this silence the centerpiece of his campaign, refusing PAC money entirely and outraising Thompson $2.6 million to $2 million in 2025 -- a stunning deficit for an incumbent who has never received less than 61% of the vote [10].

The money trail explains the silence. Thompson has raked in close to $1 million from PACs in the current cycle alone, including $12,500 from the Blue Dog Victory Fund and $5,000 each from leadership PACs tied to Nancy Pelosi and Hakeem Jeffries [10]. Over his career, he is the single biggest recipient in Congress of campaign contributions from the alcoholic beverage industry, totaling more than $1.2 million, while simultaneously owning a vineyard that sells grapes to the same companies whose legislative agenda he promotes in Washington [3][4][11]. The insurance industry has contributed $191,358 to his campaigns -- notable for a member who sits on the Health Subcommittee overseeing healthcare policy [12]. As the Press Democrat delicately noted, "plenty of lawmakers in Washington act as advocates for particular industries," but Thompson "is in business with some of the same companies whose agendas he promotes" [4].

The bottom line on Mike Thompson is that he is a competent legislator who has taken genuinely good votes on war powers, drug pricing, and tax enforcement -- but whose quarter-century incumbency has produced exactly the kind of entrenched, PAC-funded, industry-entangled career that the 80% Bill exists to reform. He will fight for your right to affordable prescriptions. He will not fight for your right to know who is funding his campaigns, or for the term limits that would send him home. The congressman from Wine Country has been fermenting in Washington for a very long time, and he has no intention of being corked.

Michael R. Turner has represented the Dayton, Ohio area in Congress since 2003 — first in the old 3rd District, now in the redrawn 10th — making him a 23-year veteran of the institution he has never once proposed term-limiting. A former mayor of Dayton, Turner built his congressional identity around Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, the district's largest employer, and the Armed Services Committee, where he has served as a senior member for over two decades [1]. He chaired the House Intelligence Committee from 2023 until January 2025, when Speaker Mike Johnson removed him, citing what Turner described as 'concerns from Mar-a-Lago' [2] — punishment, it appeared, for the sin of supporting Ukraine aid and warning that Russian propaganda was 'being uttered on the House floor' by his own Republican colleagues [3].

Turner's relationship with the 80% Bill's twenty planks is a study in selective hawkishness. He has been a forceful advocate for military spending — securing $54.7 million in earmarks for the Dayton area, enough to place him in the House's '$50 million club' [4] — but has shown no interest in auditing the Pentagon, which has never passed a financial audit. He championed the reauthorization of FISA Section 702 surveillance powers over the objections of civil-liberties advocates in both parties [5], yet has taken no public position on the DISCLOSE Act, banning dark money, or overturning Citizens United. He voted against the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act along with every other House Republican in 2021 [6]. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, impose term limits, ban corporate PACs, guarantee paid family leave, or close the carried interest loophole.

The money tells the rest of the story. Turner's career-long top contributors read like a defense-contractor roll call: Lockheed Martin has given him $98,000 over his career (mostly PAC money), making it his second-biggest all-time contributor [7]. L3Harris Technologies contributed $20,000 in 2020 alone, and BAE Systems added $27,750 in 2018 [8]. His donor list includes 27 billionaire contributors, among them Blackstone CEO Stephen Schwarzman ($29.7 billion net worth), Home Depot co-founder Bernard Marcus, and defense executive Eren Ozmen of Sierra Nevada Corp [9]. Meanwhile, a Politico investigation found he spent over $70,000 in campaign funds on restaurant meals across 370 food-related expenses in Dayton, Washington, London, and Belgium since 2017 [10].

Turner's political brand is the defense-establishment Republican who occasionally bucks his party on foreign policy but never on domestic reform. He certified the 2020 election results and refused to sign the Texas v. Pennsylvania amicus brief [11] — acts of institutional courage that earned him a purge from his committee chairmanship. But courage on Ukraine does not extend to courage on stock-trading bans, dark-money disclosure, or Pentagon accountability. After 23 years, Dayton voters might reasonably ask: if not now, when?

Glenn "GT" Thompson is the chairman of the House Agriculture Committee, a descendant of dairy farmers, and a man who voted against his own son's right to marry three days before attending that son's wedding. If you need a single image to understand how Washington works, it is a congressman toasting his gay son's marriage on Saturday after voting to deny it legal protection on Tuesday, then having his spokesperson call the whole thing an "election-year messaging stunt."

Thompson has represented rural Pennsylvania since 2009 and chairs one of the most lobbied committees in Congress. Since becoming chairman in 2023, his PAC donations have surged — one of every seven dollars in PAC contributions during the first half of 2023 came from companies that had never given to him before. In the 2023-2024 cycle, he raised $2.86 million total, with $1.5 million of that coming from PACs and party money. The crop production and agricultural processing industries alone poured over $600,000 into his campaign, and the pesticide industry has been particularly generous, contributing over $600,000 in 2023-2024 including $9,200 directly from Bayer — the company whose flagship weedkiller Roundup has generated billions in cancer-related lawsuits.

In return, Thompson has delivered. His farm bill draft includes a provision that would make it functionally impossible to sue pesticide manufacturers by codifying EPA labeling as the sole standard and stripping states of the right to require additional warnings. He calls it "protecting American agriculture." Consumer advocates call it the Monsanto Protection Act on steroids. His farm bill also slashes $30 billion from SNAP benefits over a decade, cutting food assistance for 40 million people — including 17 million children and 6 million older adults — while expanding subsidized crop insurance and creating new loopholes for "precision agriculture" equipment purchases that funnel money to the largest agribusiness operations.

On the 80% Bill's planks, Thompson is a near-total miss. He voted against certifying Pennsylvania's 2020 electoral votes. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, auditing the Pentagon, ending dark money, or imposing term limits. He has not cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act. He has not championed paid family leave. His idea of protecting farmland is making sure Bayer can spray whatever it wants on it without getting sued.

Thompson is what happens when a committee chairman's donor list and legislative agenda become indistinguishable. He will tell you he is fighting for family farmers while cashing checks from the corporations that put family farmers out of business. And if you catch the contradiction, his office will assure you it is just an election-year messaging stunt.

Dina Titus has represented Las Vegas in Congress since 2009 -- long enough to co-chair both the Congressional Gaming Caucus and the Congressional Travel and Tourism Caucus [1], which is a bit like being the official biographer of the people who write your checks. Her top career industry contributors read like a Vegas Strip directory: casinos and gambling, lodging and tourism, and real estate, with MGM Resorts International a perennial top donor [2]. In Nevada's 1st district, which covers the heart of Las Vegas, this is less corruption than ecosystem.

Titus has championed hospitality-sector legislation, including an apprenticeship bill designed to help hotels and casinos build their workforce pipeline [3]. She entered 2026 sitting on a war chest of $841,000 -- more cash on hand than she's ever had at this point in a cycle [4] -- suggesting the industry pipeline flows both ways.

On the 80% Bill's reform agenda, Titus's record has bright spots on policing and voting rights. She was a cosponsor of the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which includes the body camera mandate that maps directly to Article 12 of the bill [5]. She voted for the Freedom to Vote Act and the John Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act, aligning with the bill's fair elections provisions [6]. She was among the 220 House Democrats who voted for H.R.1, the For the People Act, which included sweeping campaign finance and anti-gerrymandering reforms [7].

But the structural money-in-politics reforms? That's where the record gets quiet. Titus has not cosponsored the leading bills to ban congressional stock trading, impose a lifetime lobbying ban, or ban corporate PACs [8]. She declined to participate in the 2024 Political Courage Test from Vote Smart, refusing to share her positions on key issues with voters [9]. For a member who's been in Congress for over fifteen years, that silence speaks volumes.

Titus is not hostile to reform -- she's simply comfortable. She represents a safe Democratic seat in a city built on the understanding that the house always wins. The 80% Bill asks whether the house should be Congress or the American people. Titus, co-chair of the Gaming Caucus, might find that question uncomfortably on-the-nose.

Paul Tonko is the kind of Democrat who shows up to every town hall, votes the right way on nearly every bill, and still manages to be the most anonymous seventeen-term member of Congress you've never heard of. Representing New York's 20th district — Albany and its suburbs — Tonko has carved out a career as the ranking member of the Energy and Commerce Subcommittee on Environment, Manufacturing, and Critical Materials, a title so long it functions as its own filibuster.

To his considerable credit, Tonko checks more boxes on the 80% Bill than most of his colleagues. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act and its historic Medicare drug negotiation provisions, then went on a multi-stop tour of his district extolling its virtues with the enthusiasm of a man who finally got to see the policy he'd been pushing for a decade become law. He championed the PFAS Action Act through the House, fighting Republican attempts to exempt toxic "forever chemicals" from Superfund cleanup — a genuinely important fight on an issue most Americans can't even pronounce. He voted for the For the People Act (H.R. 1), which contained the DISCLOSE Act's dark money provisions and campaign finance reforms that align squarely with multiple 80% Bill planks.

On the Epstein files, Tonko has been surprisingly vocal. At a February 2026 town hall in Ballston Spa, he called the redacted releases "a cover-up" and demanded authorization to view unredacted files with his colleagues, declaring he wanted to see "what they're trying to redact." At the same event, he called for abolishing ICE — a position that takes genuine political courage even in a safe blue district.

Where Tonko falls short is in the arena of money. His campaign finance record, while modest by congressional standards, still relies on the PAC-and-lobbyist pipeline that the 80% Bill would dismantle. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, and his position on corporate PAC bans remains conspicuously undefined. For a man who rails against dark money in elections, the absence of a personal pledge to refuse corporate PAC contributions is the kind of gap that separates the progressive faithful from the progressive performative.

On the remaining planks — term limits, banning corporate home buying, right to repair, junk fees, auditing the Pentagon, taxing the ultra-wealthy, and funding Social Security — Tonko's record ranges from quiet support to complete silence. His 0.7% missed-vote rate over seventeen years proves he's physically present for virtually every roll call. The question is whether showing up and voting reliably is enough, or whether the 80% Bill demands the kind of loud, aggressive championing that Tonko reserves exclusively for environmental subcommittee hearings and PFAS briefings.

Mark Takano taught British literature in Riverside public schools for 23 years before voters sent him to Congress in 2012, making him the first openly gay person of color elected to the House [1][2]. He now represents California's 39th district and serves as Ranking Member of the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs, where he authored the PACT Act -- the most comprehensive toxic exposure legislation in a generation, extending healthcare to millions of veterans exposed to burn pits and Agent Orange [3]. He also sits on the Education and Workforce Committee, and he is perhaps best known nationally for his Thirty-Two Hour Workweek Act, which would amend the Fair Labor Standards Act to trigger overtime after 32 hours rather than 40 [4][5]. The bill has been endorsed by the AFL-CIO, SEIU, and the Economic Policy Institute, and Bernie Sanders introduced a Senate companion [6]. In short, Takano is a progressive who does progressive things, which makes the audit of his record against the 80% Bill more about structural complicity than ideological betrayal.

Takano checks a lot of the populist boxes. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act and the Build Back Better Act, supporting Medicare drug price negotiation and describing the latter as "a transformative investment in our families, our workforce, and our climate that is fully paid for by making big corporations and the wealthiest few pay their fair share" [7]. He voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq War AUMF as part of the NDAA [8]. He signed onto the bipartisan Epstein Files Transparency Act, supporting the release of documents related to convicted sex trafficker Jeffrey Epstein [9]. He introduced the Unsubscribe Act with bipartisan co-sponsors to crack down on predatory subscription fees -- a direct hit at junk fees -- declaring that "corporations are cashing in on subscription models that rely on a consumer forgetting to cancel a free trial" [10]. And he co-introduced the Stop Wall Street Landlords Act with Ro Khanna and Katie Porter, targeting institutional investors gobbling up single-family rental homes and calling for hearings on the flood of corporate money into the housing market [11][12].

Where it gets complicated is the territory between rhetoric and structure. Takano has slammed Republican tax bills as "nothing more than a cruel giveaway to the powerful at the expense of everyday Americans" that "shower the ultra-wealthy with new tax breaks" [13]. Fair enough. But he has served in Congress since 2013 without co-sponsoring a congressional stock trading ban or a corporate PAC ban, and the carried interest loophole he presumably opposes has survived every Congress he has served in. On the Pentagon audit -- the Department of Defense has failed its audit eight consecutive times, spending over $800 billion annually with no clean accounting -- Takano's Veterans' Affairs perch gives him a front-row seat, yet his focus has been on the VA side of the ledger rather than confronting the broader defense spending apparatus [14][15]. He is a reliable vote for the right things when they come to the floor, but not typically the one forcing them there.

The money is instructive. Takano's campaign has been funded heavily by labor unions, public sector unions, and education interests -- natural allies for a former teacher and progressive Democrat [16]. His top contributing sectors over his career have included public sector unions, teachers unions, and labor organizations, with lawyers and health professionals rounding out the list [16]. This is cleaner money than most, and his fundraising totals are modest by congressional standards. But the structural point remains: even a relatively principled member operates within a system where PAC money from interested parties flows to the lawmakers who oversee those interests, and nobody in Congress seems in any particular hurry to dismantle that arrangement. Takano is one of the better ones -- the PACT Act alone is a genuine legislative achievement -- but "one of the better ones" is grading on a curve that the 80% Bill exists to flatten.

Norma Torres is the only Central American-born member of Congress, and she has never let anyone forget it -- nor should she. Born in Escuintla, Guatemala, in 1965, she was sent to live with relatives in Southern California at age five to escape a civil war that would eventually kill 200,000 people. She worked as a 911 dispatcher for the LAPD for seventeen years, often on the graveyard shift four floors underground, before entering politics through the Pomona City Council, the California State Assembly, and the State Senate [1][2]. She has represented the Inland Empire's 35th District since 2015, sitting on the House Appropriations Committee and the Subcommittee on State and Foreign Operations, where she has become the leading congressional voice on Central American anti-corruption policy [3][4]. Her district is home to over 200,000 transportation and warehouse workers who move trillions of dollars in goods from the ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach [5]. Torres knows what it means to work the night shift, which is more than most of her colleagues can say.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Torres has been notably active on several fronts. She offered an amendment to the Republican-backed congressional stock trading bill to close a loophole allowing members to reinvest dividends, calling the GOP version a "political scam" by "Republicans pretending to care about corruption" [6][7]. She co-sponsored the Justice in Policing Act of 2020, which included requirements for federal officers to wear body cameras, and she secured $800,000 in federal funding for body cameras for the Montclair Police Department in her own district [8][9]. She introduced the Repairing Social Security After Trump and DOGE Act to shore up the program's finances [10]. Through her appropriations work, she has been a consistent advocate for transparency and anti-corruption measures, securing $61.5 million for anti-corruption efforts in Central America's Northern Triangle and successfully pushing the State Department to create a public blacklist of corrupt Central American officials [4][11].

Where Torres aligns with the 80% Bill but lacks a leading role is on the structural reform planks -- term limits, the DISCLOSE Act, overturning Citizens United, banning corporate PACs, and closing the carried interest loophole. These are areas where she has generally voted with her caucus when bills reached the floor, co-sponsoring the For the People Act and supporting campaign finance reform in principle, but she has not been the one drafting the legislation or giving the floor speeches [12]. On paid family leave, Medicare drug negotiation, and the Inflation Reduction Act, she has been a reliable yes vote without being a marquee champion. The same pattern holds on the AUMF repeals and Pentagon audit -- supportive in the roll call, absent from the press release.

Torres' fundraising is modest by congressional standards but follows familiar patterns. Her campaign relies on a mix of individual donors and PAC contributions, with labor unions -- particularly miscellaneous unions tracked by OpenSecrets -- among her top organizational supporters, consistent with a district built on warehouse labor and logistics [13][14]. She sits on the Appropriations Committee, which makes her a magnet for interest groups seeking earmarks and federal funding. Her 2024 cycle showed continued reliance on PAC-to-PAC transfers and leadership PAC support common among Democratic incumbents [14]. The dollar amounts are smaller than many colleagues, but the structural dependency is the same.

The story of Norma Torres is not one of dramatic hypocrisy but of familiar limitation. She is a former 911 dispatcher who genuinely understands working-class life, who has used her appropriations seat to fight corruption abroad with a specificity and tenacity that few in Congress can match, and who has taken real action on stock trading loopholes and police accountability. But on the systemic reforms that would reshape how Congress itself operates -- dark money disclosure, corporate PAC bans, term limits -- she is a vote, not a voice. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives will champion the consensus agenda. Torres will sign her name to most of it. Whether she will spend political capital on the parts that threaten incumbents like her is another question entirely.

Sen. Thom Tillis is Washington's most fascinating contradiction: a Republican senator censured by his own state party for being too bipartisan [1], who simultaneously collected more pharmaceutical industry money than nearly every other member of Congress [2]. Now that he has announced he will not seek reelection in 2026 -- one day after voting against Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" and drawing the president's wrath [3] -- Tillis enters his final year as that rarest of creatures: a lame duck with nothing left to lose and, historically, very little inclination to use it.

To be fair, Tillis has broken with his party on genuinely consequential votes. He supported the Respect for Marriage Act, repealing the Defense of Marriage Act [4]. He voted for the Bipartisan Safer Communities Act, funding state red flag laws [5]. He co-authored immigration legislation offering a pathway to citizenship for DACA recipients [6]. These votes earned him a formal censure from the North Carolina GOP in 2023, which tells you something about the state of the party and, perhaps, something about Tillis.

But the pharmaceutical money tells a different story. A Kaiser Health News analysis found Tillis received more than $156,000 from drug manufacturer PACs in 2019 alone -- more than any other member of Congress that year [7]. Between his campaign and leadership PAC, he took in over $408,000 from the pharmaceutical industry, with 78% from industry PACs [2]. Within two weeks of co-sponsoring the "Lower Costs, More Cures Act" in 2019, he accepted over $20,000 from pharma PACs [8]. That bill conveniently omitted the inflation cap on drug prices that the competing Grassley bill included -- the one provision Big Pharma hated most.

On the 80% Bill's platform, Tillis's record is mixed at best. He has shown no support for banning congressional stock trading (Article 1), the lifetime lobbying ban (Article 3), banning corporate PACs (Article 5), or Pentagon audits (Article 6). He opposed the Freedom to Vote Act (Article 8) and the DISCLOSE Act (Article 16). On taxing the ultra-wealthy (Article 4) and closing the carried interest loophole (Article 17), he has voted no. His missed-vote rate of 7% -- nearly triple the Senate median -- suggests that even when he is in Washington, he is not always working [9].

Tillis once said that "leaders who are willing to embrace bipartisanship, compromise, and demonstrate independent thinking are becoming an endangered species" [3]. He is right. But an endangered species that takes $400,000 from pharma while shaping drug pricing legislation is still, at the end of the day, a well-funded one.
